critic_raw:
1. Preprocessing leakage: pixel normalisation statistics (mean, std) are computed on the entire dataset before splitting. For a hospital-site-stratified split, this means the normalization parameters encode the intensity distribution of test-set images acquired at specific sites. This is especially harmful here because site-to-site variation in microscopy protocols (staining intensity, illumination, scanner calibration) is the primary source of distribution shift the model needs to handle. Normalizing out this variation before splitting masks the difficulty of cross-site generalisation.

2. The model is re-trained on the combined training + validation set (9,500 images) after hyperparameter selection, then evaluated on the test set. This means the model evaluated on the test set has never seen its own validation set performance — the selected hyperparameters were tuned against a 1,500-image validation set, but the final model uses a different distribution of 9,500 images. If the validation set happened to contain more hard cases from a particular site, the selected architecture may be mismatched to the easier combined training set. More fundamentally, re-training on combined data after selection inflates the test set estimate because the test set is now the only true hold-out.

3. The logistic regression baseline uses sensitivity as the tuning criterion with 5-fold cross-validation on the training set only, while the CNN uses early stopping on the validation set sensitivity. These are different validation procedures: cross-validation on training data versus a fixed hold-out. If the training data contains a different class distribution per fold than the validation set, the two models are tuned against different statistical targets, making the comparison unfair. The design claims "both models receive equivalent care" but the tuning procedures are structurally different.

4. The labeling procedure re-labels only 500 randomly selected images from the 9,500 originally single-labeled images. Sensitivity analysis is then reported "on consensus-labeled vs. single-labeled subsets." But the test set was constructed by stratified random sampling, meaning the 1,500 test images contain ~75–80 consensus-labeled images. Conclusions about whether the CNN advantage holds under better labeling are drawn from a 75-image consensus test set — a sample too small for reliable sensitivity estimates and too small for meaningful inter-model comparison.

5. The primary metric (sensitivity) is reported without specifying the operating threshold, and no threshold selection procedure is described for the test set evaluation. If both models' thresholds are independently optimized on the validation set to maximize sensitivity, and sensitivity is then reported at those thresholds, the comparison is not at a matched operating point — a model with higher recall at threshold 0.3 may have lower recall at threshold 0.5. Without a fixed threshold or fixed specificity constraint, sensitivity comparisons across models are not interpretable.
all_issues_raised: Pixel normalisation statistics computed on full dataset before splitting leaks site-specific intensity distributions into training features
Re-training on combined train+validation after hyperparameter selection means selected architecture may be mismatched to training distribution
CNN and logistic regression use structurally different tuning procedures (fixed hold-out vs. cross-validation), making the comparison unfair
Consensus-labeled test subset is ~75 images, too small for reliable sensitivity comparisons between models
Primary metric (sensitivity) reported without a fixed operating threshold or specificity constraint, making cross-model comparisons uninterpretable
Pixel normalization and handcrafted feature standardization statistics fit on full dataset before splitting, leaking test set statistics into training normalization
Re-training on combined train+validation after model selection creates a disconnect between the selected hyperparameters (optimized for 7,000 images) and the final model (trained on 9,500 images)
Consensus re-labeling limited to 500 images does not validate reliability of the 9,500 single-pathologist labels on which the model was trained
Hospital-site stratified splitting ensures all sites appear in training, preventing evaluation of generalization to new unseen hospital sites
Early stopping based on validation sensitivity (a threshold-dependent metric) risks overfitting the decision boundary to the validation set class distribution
Normalization statistics computed on full dataset before splitting — cross-site intensity normalization masks site-specific variation and inflates apparent generalization
Retraining on train+validation after hyperparameter selection produces a different model than what was selected — test evaluation is of an unselected model
Consensus relabeling of 500 random images yields ~75 test-set images with consensus labels — insufficient statistical power for the proposed sensitivity analysis
Primary metric (sensitivity) is underspecified — no fixed threshold or specificity constraint stated for model selection or final evaluation
